title: Modal thinking - Separate decision making and execution
html:
The idea is simple but freeing: keep deciding and doing in two separate modes, and never let them run at the same time.

Decision Mode:

  • Empirical: lean on what decisions (avoidance included) made you regret before. Your own history is the cheapest data you have.
  • An arbitrary decision is still hundredfold better than an impulsive driven one. A choice you can stand behind beats a reaction you were dragged into.

Execution Mode:

  • You have no other option. Once you’ve decided, the deciding is over—there is nothing left to weigh.
  • You don’t stop in the middle.
  • Whether it’s a great or bad move is up to be judge by the Decision Mode’s reflection, later, with a calm head—not mid-stride.
  • Even it turns out to be a bad decision, the outcome of strengthen your adherence, self-worth, and trust in the heuristic is long term yield. You are training the muscle of following through, and that compounds.

This way, you saved it tons of mental energy from bounced thinking—the exhausting loop of reconsidering a choice while you’re supposed to be acting on it. (A pattern I first picked up from a friend’s notes.)

  • Programmable Self
